cards.title: Sunbath — Types & Technique
cards._legacyHtml:
Types:
  • Full sunbath: Entire body exposed; 10–30 min; morning sun preferred (07:00–09:00 hrs).
  • Partial sunbath: Specific region exposed; used in debilitated patients.
  • Sand bath: Patient buried in warm beach/river sand; promotes sweating and mineral absorption.
  • Air + sun bath: Combined — removes clothing in fresh air and sunlight simultaneously.
Best time: Morning (before 10 AM) or late afternoon (after 4 PM). Avoid 10 AM–4 PM in tropical climates.
Positioning: Expose back first, then front. Equal time each side.

cards.title: Vitamin D Protocol
cards._legacyHtml:
UV-B Mechanism: UV-B (290–315 nm) converts 7-dehydrocholesterol in skin to previtamin D3 → cholecalciferol (D3) → hepatic 25-OH-D3 → renal 1,25(OH)2D3 (calcitriol — active form).
Protocol for Vit D deficiency: 20–30 min full body sun, 3–5×/week, 10 AM–3 PM window (when UVI ≥3). Arms and legs exposed (40% BSA).
Target: Serum 25-OH-D3: 50–80 ng/mL.
Supplemental: D3 2000–4000 IU/day with K2 100 mcg. Recheck in 8–12 weeks.
SPF note: SPF 15+ blocks 99% UV-B synthesis.

cards.title: Air Bath (Aerotherapy)
cards._legacyHtml:
Definition: Therapeutic exposure of the body to fresh open air without clothing (partial or full).
Physiological Effects: Stimulates skin nerve endings, improves thermoregulation, enhances vasomotor tone, increases pO2, reduces stress hormones.
Protocol: Start with 5–10 min in a sheltered outdoor area; gradually increase to 30–45 min over 2 weeks. Comfortable temperature: 20–25°C. Combine with deep breathing exercises.
Indications: Nervous debility, skin diseases, respiratory conditions, insomnia, anaemia.
Contraindications: Fever, cold/damp climate, joint inflammation.

cards.title: Forest Bathing — Shinrin-Yoku
cards._legacyHtml:
Definition: Mindful immersion in a forest environment — engaging all five senses without physical exertion.
Active Agents: Phytoncides (α-pinene, d-limonene) — volatile organic compounds emitted by trees; negative ions; natural light variation.
Evidence: ↓ cortisol, ↓ adrenaline, ↓ BP, ↓ HR, ↑ NK cell activity (anti-cancer), ↑ DHEA, improved mood and sleep.
Protocol: 2–4 hour walk, 2 km distance, no phones. Focus on sounds, smells, textures. Deep nasal breathing.
Frequency: Once weekly for significant NK cell effect (lasts 7–30 days).

cards.title: Bright Light Therapy (Phototherapy)
cards._legacyHtml:
Device: 10,000 lux light box (full-spectrum, UV-filtered).
Mechanism: Suppresses nocturnal melatonin, resets suprachiasmatic nucleus (SCN), normalises circadian cortisol rhythm.
Protocol: 30 min daily, within 1 hour of waking. Sit 40–50 cm from box. Eyes open (do not stare directly). Avoid after 4 PM.
Response: SAD response within 2–4 days; antidepressant effect in 1–2 weeks.
Indications: SAD, non-seasonal depression (adjunct), circadian rhythm disorders, jet lag, shift-work disorder, winter fatigue.
Contraindications: Bipolar disorder (without mood stabiliser), retinal disease, photosensitising drugs.

cards.title: SAD — Seasonal Affective Disorder Protocol
cards._legacyHtml:
BNYS Protocol for SAD:
1. Bright Light Therapy: 10,000 lux, 30 min/day, on waking. Start October–November in temperate regions.
2. Dawn Simulation: Gradually increasing light (30–40 min) before scheduled wake time — effective as BLT for mild SAD.
3. Heliotherapy: Daily outdoor morning walk (20–30 min) before 10 AM, even on overcast days.
4. Nutritional: Vit D3 2000–4000 IU/day, Omega-3 2g/day, Magnesium glycinate 300 mg/night.
5. Yoga: Surya Namaskar (12 rounds AM), Bhastrika pranayama 5 min.
6. Review: Reassess every 2 weeks; titrate BLT duration.

cards.title: Heliotherapy — Contraindications & Safety
cards._legacyHtml:
Contraindications:
  • Active tuberculosis (pulmonary — systemic heating may worsen)
  • Lupus (SLE — photosensitive)
  • Porphyria
  • Xeroderma pigmentosum
  • Severe cardiac or renal failure
  • High fever
  • Photosensitising medications (doxycycline, amiodarone, chlorpromazine)
Safety Rules: Always protect eyes (UV-blocking sunglasses). Hydrate before sun exposure. Monitor skin for erythema. Discontinue if sunburn occurs. Use minimum erythema dose (MED) as guide for sensitive patients.
